food_name: Oyakodon (Japanese Chicken and Egg Rice Bowl)

local_names.tamil: ஜப்பான் கோழி மற்றும் முட்டை சாத கிண்ணம்

aliases: Japanese chicken and egg rice bowl
ஜப்பான் கோழி மற்றும் முட்டை சாத கிண்ணம்

english: Japanese chicken and egg rice bowl
tamil: ஜப்பான் கோழி மற்றும் முட்டை சாத கிண்ணம்

food_subgroup: Japanese donburi — chicken thigh and onion simmered in dashi-mirin-soy broth, finished with runny custard egg, served over steamed short-grain rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Oyakodon. Closest parent is a Japanese donburi rice bowl with chicken and egg topping. No exact URI available; gap documented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Japanese chicken donburi: diced chicken thigh and onion in dashi-mirin-soy broth with custard egg topping over steamed short-grain rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0BDF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for Japanese rice bowl dishes; no exact Oyakodon code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Oyakodon or Japanese donburi rice bowl as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Oyakodon weighs approximately 480g, comprising 220g steamed Japanese short-grain rice, 180g chicken and egg mixture, and 80g dashi-mirin-soy broth.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken thigh cooked, White rice cooked short-grain, Whole egg cooked, Soy sauce, Onion cooked; composite recipe calculation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Oyakodon is not listed as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken thigh (skin removed), cooked Japanese short-grain white rice, whole cooked egg, soy sauce, mirin, and cooked onion, in proportions consistent with a standard 480g bowl (220g rice, 180g chicken-egg mixture, 80g broth). Values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for regional validation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Japanese short-grain white rice GI ~72–78; Oyakodon GL estimated from 480g serving (90.2g available carbohydrate × GI 72 / 100 = 65.0)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Oyakodon as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source — Japanese short-grain white rice — which has a published GI of 72–78 in the University of Sydney GI Database. Chicken fat and egg protein in the dish provide modest GI moderation relative to plain rice; GI is conservatively set at the lower bound of 72. Glycemic load per 480g serving calculated as: (18.8g carbohydrate per 100g × 480g / 100) × 72 / 100 = 90.24g available carbohydrate × 0.72 = 64.97, rounded to 64.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 398mg per 100g; a standard 480g bowl delivers approximately 1,910mg sodium, approaching the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce (少めのタレ) when ordering Oyakodon, as the dashi-soy broth is the primary sodium source; choosing a smaller rice portion further reduces total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) from Japanese short-grain rice and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~65) from 90g available carbohydrate in a standard 480g bowl
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a small rice portion (小盛り) to reduce glycemic load; the egg and chicken protein in Oyakodon provide meaningful GI moderation compared to plain rice dishes such as gyudon, and requesting extra egg topping further improves the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate cholesterol (92mg/100g) and saturated fat (1.5g/100g) from egg yolk and chicken thigh; high sodium from soy-dashi broth is an additional cardiovascular risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce sodium and cholesterol load; the egg in Oyakodon contributes beneficial choline, selenium (22mcg/100g), and vitamin D (1.2mcg/100g), which partially offset cardiovascular risk when consumed in moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-GI rice component is mildly proinflammatory; egg provides anti-inflammatory selenium (22mcg/100g), vitamin D (1.2mcg/100g), and choline
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile of the egg and chicken components; reducing the rice portion and increasing the egg-to-rice ratio is advisable to lower the proinflammatory glycemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyakodon (親子丼) — whose name translates as 'parent and child bowl' because chicken (the parent) and egg (the child) are cooked together — is one of Japan's most beloved donburi (rice bowl) dishes. Diced chicken thigh and sliced onion are simmered in a shallow pan with a sweet-savoury dashi broth made from dashi stock, mirin, soy sauce, sake, and a touch of sugar until the chicken is just cooked and the onion is translucent. Beaten eggs are then poured over and cooked over low heat with minimal stirring until barely set in a loose, silky, custard-like consistency — the defining textural signature of Oyakodon. This soft, quivering egg-and-chicken mixture is slid directly over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain rice and garnished with mitsuba (Japanese parsley) or spring onion. The flavour is gently sweet, savoury, and deeply comforting. Per 100g, Oyakodon provides 158 kcal, 11.5g protein, 5.0g fat, and 18.8g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of selenium (22mcg), vitamin B12 (0.8mcg), niacin (5.8mg), and phosphorus (165mg). Sodium is high at 398mg/100g due to soy sauce and dashi. The GI is 72 (high) driven by Japanese short-grain rice; a standard 480g bowl carries a glycemic load of approximately 65. Clinically, the egg protein and chicken make Oyakodon a more balanced donburi than gyudon from a protein-to-carbohydrate perspective. In Singapore, Oyakodon is widely available at Japanese chain restaurants, food courts, and Japanese home-style restaurants.

confidence_reason: Oyakodon is not listed as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy component entries (cooked chicken thigh, cooked Japanese short-grain rice, whole cooked egg, soy sauce, mirin, cooked onion) in standard proportions, cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA data. The input record values are consistent with this composite and have been retained. Confidence is set to medium rather than high because no single approved-source entry for Oyakodon as a complete dish exists.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Oyakodon as a composite dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026; individual component fatty acid data exist but composite allocation is not reported. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Oyakodon as a composite dish exists in the approved GI source. Glycemic load per 480g serving = (18.8 × 480 / 100) × 72 / 100 = 90.24 × 0.72 = 64.97, reported as 64.9. FoodOn URI is null — no dedicated FoodOn concept for Oyakodon exists; closest parent is Japanese donburi rice bowl. FoodEx2 code A0BDF is used as closest parent; no exact Oyakodon code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Hindi local name is null — no standard Hindi rendering of Oyakodon is in common use; this does not affect G6 compliance as this is a Japanese dish (mandatory language: Japanese only).
